raw_case:
Cavernous hemangioma of the rib mimicking malignant bone tumor: A case report
Intraosseous hemangiomas are benign vascular tumors which are more commonly seen in the vertebrae and skull but they rare in the ribs. Rib hemangiomas can mimic malignant rib lesions and definitive clinical and imaging diagnosis might be difficult and pathological confirmation will be required. Here we report a rib hemangioma in a 49-year-old male presented with painless chest wall swelling of 9 years duration, on chest CT scan the lesion showed dense radiating type calcification with cortical destruction. The patient was sent for core needle biopsy with the impression of chondrosarcoma, subjected to histopathology and confirmed to be a hemangioma. So, although rib hemangioma is rare it should be at the back of our mind for differential diagnosis of aggressive appearing rib lesions especially in patients presented with indolent, painless and incidental rib swelling.

Introduction

Intraosseous hemangiomas are benign vascular malformation which are more commonly seen in the vertebrae and skull accounting for 75%, the remaining in long bones, short tubular bones and ribs [1]. Based on their microscopic appearance they are classified in to 4 types cavernous, capillary, arteriovenous and venous intraosseous hemangiomaSkeletal cavernous hemangioma is a rare benign tumor which accounts for 0.7% of primary bone tumors, more common in the spine and skull [2]. Rib cavernous hemangiomas are very rare benign tumors but might be confused with malignant rib tumors like multiple myeloma, metastasis and chondrosarcoma as they may exhibit local invasiveness, and should be considered in the differential diagnosis of asymptomatic long standing rib tumors [3,4].Different imaging modalities including CT, MRI and PET are used in narrowing differential diagnosis but histopathology is needed for making a definitive diagnosis.

Case presentation

Here we present a case of a 49-year-old male who presented with long standing right chest wall swelling of 9 years duration. The swelling increased gradually in size but its painless. There is no overlying skin discoloration. Otherwise, he has no history of weight loss, fever and discharge from swelling. He does not have swelling on other sites, no prior trauma or surgical history. No family history of similar illness or other pertinent conditions. For this he visited a local health center and chest X-ray was done which showed right lower chest wall densely calcified lesion arising centered over the 9th rib. Then he was referred to a referral hospital in the vicinity. Chest computed tomography was done there, which showed expansile lesion arising from right 9th rib having internal dense radiating type calcification (Fig. 1, Fig. 2A and B). Then he was referred to our hospital with impression of chondrosarcoma for confirmation and management. Up on arrival, on physical examination vital signs were with in normal range. There was right lower anterior chest wall non mobile non tender mass measuring 5cm in maximum transverse dimension. On investigation complete blood count was in normal limit. Renal and liver function tests were normal. ESR was normal.Imaging– (unfortunately we could not get the chest x ray). He has pre and post contrast chest CT scan (attached below)Then ultrasound guided core needle biopsy was done.Histopathology slide and result (Fig. 3) showed rib hemangioma. There was no complication like bleeding during the procedure and the patient was observed for 3 hours and sent home. After confirmation of this benign tumor, he has followed up at the outpatient patient clinic.

Discussion

Rib hemangioma is very rare benign vascular tumor, which presents around fifth decade of life and there is slight female predominance [5]. In a systematic review of case reports by Kumar J et al showed female to male ration of 1.69:1, more common over mid thoracic ribs fourth to eighth and over left side hemithorax. Size of the lesions was significantly variable [4]. In our case the patient is male with right side lower thoracic region lesion which is implies slight variation from reported cases.Most cases are asymptomatic and incidentally detected on chest x rays done for other indications. Uncommonly it might present with pain, swelling and pathological fracture and rarely may present with thoracic outlet syndrome and compressive paraparesis if it arises from first rib or adjacent to thoracic vertebrae respectively [4,6]. In our case the presentation was long standing painless chest wall swelling.The usual imaging pattern of rib hemangioma is well defined, expansile intraosseous lesion with internal osteolytic areas and coarsened trabeculae showing honey comb appearance without cortical destruction. Occasionally sunburst type calcification may be seen although this feature is more commonly seen in skull hemangioma. Cortical destruction can also be seen, in these cases it might be confused with malignant tumors [4,6,7]. In our case the lesion showed the less commonly seen findings- sunburst calcification and cortical destruction and that is why malignant lesion was considered initially.Degree of cortical destruction and lesion size can be better evaluated using CT and MRI. When it shows aggressive features with sunburst appearance or cortical disruption with extra osseous extension it mimics malignant tumors [5]. So based on the imaging pattern the differential diagnosis includes both malignant rib tumors such as Ewing sarcoma, chondrosarcoma, and myeloma or benign rib tumors like osteochondroma, fibrous dysplasia, eosinophilic granuloma, and aneurysmal bone cyst [2,8].Diagnosis of rib hemangioma is delayed due to low degree of suspicion as its rare and have aforementioned variable imaging features [8].But on imaging it is mostly difficult to differentiate from malignant rib lesions like chondrosarcoma, multiple myeloma and metastatic tumors [5]. MRI plays a supplementary role to CT scan, on T1 weighted sequence medullary fat will demonstrate high signal intensity and on T2 weighted sequence will show bright large multiple vessels [7]. On functional imaging like FDG PET scan it will show low up take compared to the avid uptake by malignant lesions [4].Definite diagnosis is only possible from histopathology [6]. In our case diagnosis was biopsy confirmed. There is no strong consensus about the preoperative percutaneous needle biopsy, as there may be risk of bleeding if it's a hemangioma or needle tract seeding in cases of malignant tumors unless multiple myeloma or metastatic disease are considered [2,8].The recommended standard management is surgical resection and, if its large and risk of hemorrhage is high preoperative embolization can be considered. But it can be managed conservatively if definitive imaging diagnosis is possible or if it's a biopsy proven case [4].In our case the patient is being managed conservatively.

Conclusion

This case report highlights the importance of considering rib hemangioma in the differential diagnosis of rib tumors. Although its rare and a benign tumor it can be mistaken for malignant rib lesion as it can have radiologically aggressive appearance and difficult to differentiate from lesions like chondrosarcoma, metastasis and multiple myeloma. And this will have a significant effect on patient management. Definitive diagnosis is via histopathology.

Authors’ contributions

All authors contributed to the conduct of this research and read and approved the final version of the manuscript.

Ethics approval and consent to participate

Not applicable.

Availability of data and materials

The data supporting the findings of the case are available upon request to the corresponding author.

Patient consent

Written informed consent was obtained from the patient for anonymized patient information to be published in this article.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information**: 49-year-old male.
- **Chief Complaint**: Painless chest wall swelling of 9 years duration.
- **History of Present Illness**: Painless, gradually enlarging right chest wall swelling for 9 years, with no overlying skin discoloration. No associated systemic symptoms such as weight loss, fever, or discharge. No other swellings elsewhere. No history of trauma or prior surgical intervention.
- **Past Medical History**: None.
- **Personal History**: None provided.
- **Family History**: None.
- **Physical Examination**: Right lower anterior chest wall mass, non-mobile, non-tender, measuring 5 cm. Vital signs were within normal limits.
- **Ancillary Tests**:
  - Chest X-ray: Densely calcified lesion centered over the right 9th rib.
  - Chest CT scan: Expansile lesion from the right 9th rib with dense radiating calcifications (sunburst pattern), cortical destruction.
  - Bloodwork: Complete blood count, renal function, liver function tests, and ESR were normal.
  - Core needle biopsy: Histopathological confirmation of rib hemangioma (cavernous type).
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Initial Clinical Suspicion**:
   - Based on the painless, longstanding chest wall mass, and imaging findings of cortical destruction with sunburst calcifications, a malignant etiology such as chondrosarcoma was initially suspected.

2. **Differential Diagnosis Considered**:
   - **Malignant lesions**:
     - **Chondrosarcoma**: A primary consideration due to imaging findings of radiating calcification and cortical destruction.
     - **Multiple Myeloma** and **Metastatic Tumors**: Considered due to expansile rib lesion but less likely given the absence of systemic symptoms like weight loss or multiple lesions elsewhere.
   - **Benign lesions**:
     - **Osteochondroma** and **Fibrous Dysplasia**: Typically lack cortical destruction or aggressive calcifications.
     - **Rib Hemangioma**: Rarely considered initially due to the rarity and atypical features like cortical destruction and sunburst calcifications in this case.

3. **Rationale for Workup**:
   - Imaging techniques like CT highlighted the lesion’s aggressive features but were insufficient for definitive diagnosis without histological analysis.
   - Core needle biopsy and histopathology were essential for ruling out malignancy and confirming the diagnosis of rib hemangioma.
generate_case.final_diagnosis:
The final diagnosis is a **cavernous hemangioma of the rib**. This diagnosis was supported by:
   - Longstanding indolent presentation (9 years of growth without symptoms such as pain or systemic signs).
   - Imaging findings of expansile rib lesion with radiating calcifications and cortical involvement.
   - Histological confirmation via core needle biopsy, which definitively identified the lesion as a benign vascular tumor (cavernous hemangioma).
generate_case.diagnosis_results: Cavernous hemangioma of the right 9th rib.

results.thinking_process:
Okay, let me try to work through this case. So the patient is a 49-year-old male with a 9-year history of a painless chest wall swelling. The key points here are the duration, lack of pain, and gradual enlargement. No systemic symptoms like weight loss or fever, which is interesting. Also, no trauma or surgery in the past.

First, the location is the right lower anterior chest wall. The mass is non-mobile and non-tender, 5 cm. Vital signs normal. So physical exam doesn't show signs of infection or acute inflammation. 

Looking at the imaging: Chest X-ray shows a densely calcified lesion on the 9th rib. Then the CT scan adds more details: expansile lesion with sunburst calcifications and cortical destruction. Sunburst pattern is often associated with certain bone tumors. The differential for bone lesions with calcification might include osteosarcoma, chondrosarcoma, or maybe a benign tumor like osteochondroma. But cortical destruction can be a red flag for malignancy. However, the patient's long history of 9 years without systemic symptoms might suggest a slow-growing lesion, which leans towards benign.

Wait, but the biopsy result says rib hemangioma, cavernous type. Hemangiomas are usually benign vascular tumors. But how common are they in the ribs? I remember that bone hemangiomas are more often seen in the spine or skull, but ribs can be involved. Cavernous hemangioma would fit with the imaging findings of sunburst calcifications and expansion. The CT's sunburst pattern and dense calcifications might correlate with the vascular channels and phleboliths sometimes seen in hemangiomas.

But wait, cortical destruction is mentioned. That's typically a feature of aggressive or malignant tumors. How does that fit with a hemangioma? Maybe because the lesion is expansile, causing thinning and destruction of the cortex, but without actual invasion. Hemangiomas can cause bone expansion and remodeling. The biopsy is key here. If the histopathology confirms cavernous hemangioma, then even with cortical destruction, it's benign. The duration of 9 years without metastasis or other symptoms supports a benign process.

I should also consider other possibilities. Osteosarcoma would present more aggressively, with pain and rapid growth. Chondrosarcoma might have a more lobulated appearance with rings-and-arcs calcification. The biopsy result trumps imaging in this case, though. If the pathologist confirmed hemangioma, then that's the diagnosis. The core needle biopsy is reliable here if they got a good sample.

So putting it all together: long-standing painless mass, imaging shows calcified expansile rib lesion with sunburst pattern and cortical destruction, but biopsy shows cavernous hemangioma. All other labs are normal. Therefore, despite the aggressive imaging features, the diagnosis is rib hemangioma (cavernous type).
results.content:
```
### Resoning:
<step 1> The patient presents with a painless, slow-growing chest wall mass over 9 years. The absence of systemic symptoms (weight loss, fever) or skin changes suggests a benign process. Chronic, indolent growth is atypical for aggressive malignancies.  
<step 2> Imaging findings (chest X-ray and CT) reveal a densely calcified, expansile rib lesion with a sunburst calcification pattern and cortical destruction. While cortical destruction often raises concern for malignancy, the sunburst pattern can also occur in benign vascular lesions like hemangiomas, which may remodel or erode bone over time.  
<step 3> Core needle biopsy confirmed a cavernous hemangioma. Histopathology is definitive for diagnosis, overriding imaging features that might suggest malignancy. Hemangiomas, though rare in ribs, can exhibit expansive growth and calcifications (e.g., phleboliths) consistent with the observed sunburst pattern.  
<step 4> The lack of metastatic disease, normal bloodwork, and prolonged clinical stability further support the benign nature of the lesion. Alternative diagnoses (e.g., osteosarcoma, chondrosarcoma) are ruled out by biopsy and clinical history.  

### Answer:
Rib hemangioma (cavernous type)

reasoning_eval.step: The patient presents with a painless, slow-growing chest wall mass over 9 years. The absence of systemic symptoms (weight loss, fever) or skin changes suggests a benign process. Chronic, indolent growth is atypical for aggressive malignancies.

Imaging findings (chest X-ray and CT) reveal a densely calcified, expansile rib lesion with a sunburst calcification pattern and cortical destruction. While cortical destruction often raises concern for malignancy, the sunburst pattern can also occur in benign vascular lesions like hemangiomas, which may remodel or erode bone over time.

Core needle biopsy confirmed a cavernous hemangioma. Histopathology is definitive for diagnosis, overriding imaging features that might suggest malignancy. Hemangiomas, though rare in ribs, can exhibit expansive growth and calcifications (e.g., phleboliths) consistent with the observed sunburst pattern.

The lack of metastatic disease, normal bloodwork, and prolonged clinical stability further support the benign nature of the lesion. Alternative diagnoses (e.g., osteosarcoma, chondrosarcoma) are ruled out by biopsy and clinical history.

gt_reasoning_eval.step: Step 1: Initial clinical suspicion based on a painless, longstanding chest wall mass and imaging findings of cortical destruction with sunburst calcifications, leading to the initial consideration of a malignant etiology such as chondrosarcoma.

Step 2: Differential diagnosis includes malignant lesions such as chondrosarcoma and less likely multiple myeloma or metastatic tumors due to the absence of systemic symptoms, and benign lesions like osteochondroma, fibrous dysplasia, and rarely rib hemangioma.
Step 3: Imaging techniques like CT highlighted the lesion's aggressive features but were insufficient for a definitive diagnosis without histological analysis.
Step 4: Core needle biopsy and histopathology were essential for ruling out malignancy and confirming the diagnosis of rib hemangioma.
Step 5: The final diagnosis is a cavernous hemangioma of the rib, supported by a longstanding indolent presentation (9 years of growth without symptoms such as pain or systemic signs).
Step 6: Imaging findings include an expansile rib lesion with radiating calcifications and cortical involvement.
Step 7: Histological confirmation via core needle biopsy definitively identified the lesion as a benign vascular tumor (cavernous hemangioma).
